text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table. Pneumoperitoneum is established using a Veress needle via the umbilicus, followed by the insertion of an 8mm trocar in the peri-umbilical area. The abdominal cavity is inspected, revealing dense adhesions of the omentum and bowel to the anterior abdominal wall. Two additional robotic trocars are placed in the right abdomen and left upper abdomen, along with an assistant port in the left lower quadrant. The robotic system is docked from the left side.

The surgeon takes down dense inflammatory adhesions in the upper abdomen to improve visualization. Due to the severity of adhesions, the gallbladder is not safely accessible, leading to the abandonment of the cholecystectomy attempt. The surgeon visualizes part of the stomach, clears it, and creates a gastrostomy to inspect the stomach lumen. The previously placed endoscopic stent is visible, clogged with debris, which is cleared before removing the stent. The gastrostomy tract is expanded further.

The surgeon encounters brisk bleeding while gradually advancing through the stomach wall, which is temporarily controlled with pressure. Two figure-of-eight sutures are used to control the bleeding. The cystogastrotomy is expanded and reinforced with silk sutures. A running suture secures the edges, and the cavity is washed out. Necrotic material is removed from the cyst, and the anterior gastrotomy is closed with running sutures. Estimated blood loss is 300 mL.
